You can't counter this without making the platforms realise that they have more to gain from the Indian audience than they have to offer.

According to me, the biggest mistake the right wing made with Dhruv Rathee and many others like him was that they only responded to him and did not take control of the narrative.

Responders can never salvage the situation in the IW domain; it is honestly a never-ending loop for you to push across your point and the other guy keeps driving engagement from your responses.

The same thing some people of DefX are doing with Pravin Swahney guy. You are giving him the extra 50-60K views; otherwise, he is only relevant in Pakistan.

If anything needs to be amplified, it has to have its own taste and tone. If fact-checking needs to be done, it has to be done once.

Thumb rule :
Misinformation: Fact-check (Quick and fast)
Disinformation : Retaliation (With brute force)

Tata Advanced Systems Limited has rolled out the first Airbus C295 aircraft from its Final Assembly Line (FAL) facility in Vadodara, Gujarat.

This marks the first time a private Indian company has established and operationalised a complete military aircraft final assembly line in the country.

The C-295 program is being executed in partnership with Airbus for the Indian Air Force under the ₹21,000+ cR transport aircraft deal signed in 2021.

Under the program:
• First 16 aircraft are being delivered in fly-away condition from Spain
• Remaining 40 aircraft will be manufactured in India at the Vadodara facility

(📹VAMSHI goud)

tweets.title:
This tweet could elicit such a strong reply that whatever la-la land he is in right now could experience a mighty earthquake of fact-checking.

But then I saw his post criticising an IAF official for pointing out how India controlled the escalation matrix, showcased strong political will, and used indigenous weapons. I mean that's the most evident thing in the whole op apart from destroyed Pakistani bases.

He claims the most successful weapons in Op Sindoor were the S400 and Brahmos. Both have Russian involvement, but that's a very myopic view of the whole operation.

The S400 did what it was bought for with top dollars. Brahmos did its job normally. Any disciplined and potent air defense unit could have brought down at least a few Brahmos in a real-world scenario. Pakistanis are not in that category.

The most important weapon, which showcased the vision of the Indian Armed Forces along with the Indian military defence complex, was the L70.

That thing was the hero of Op Sindoor, at least as per my assessment with limited data on engagements during the conflict.

The L70 is an Indian brainchild. Many such guns exist across the world, but they were never deployed and utilised in the way the L70 was. The important thing about it is that the L70 was developed into a drone killer with a vision for a kind of threat that such a system has hardly faced or countered.

And if these guys can discount IACCS, Akashteer system and the whole group of indigenous developed radar and optical surveillance systems just to belittle the Atmanirbhar initiative, then we can assume why they are being selective.

I am very much sure that these guys get editorial space or media platforms just because either they have been around for a couple of decades or their parents were in some important position.

tweets.title:
RT by @alpha_defense: In 1945 , a New Zealand Div fought a street by street battle against the retreating German army to take the city of Trieste, Italy. 

Once the city was secured, Americans decided on a victory parade to be headed by the US Marines.

 It was pointed out that the Americans had arrived after the battle had finished & that the fighting had been done by the New Zealanders. As the Italian campaign was being run by US Army command and the parade went ahead as planned. 

In front came the US Marines, with a large banner bearing their emblem and the words ‘US Marines. Second to None’.

Behind them marched the New Zealanders carrying a large sheet with  the word ‘None’.

 It reveals deeper abt a certain American thought process that has repeated itself for decades.

“If we’re involved, we must have been decisive. If we show up, it’s our win.”

It’s not malice but a cultural operating system built on concept of  exceptionalism.
We see this pattern everywhere . In WW II, US role often overshadows the fact that Soviet Union suffered 27 million dead, Britain stood alone for years & smaller Allies like New Zealand, Australia and Canada bled for every inch of ground from North Africa to Italy to the Pacific.

In coalition ops since, Korea, Gulf or Afghanistan where multinational forces did the heavy lifting in the shadows while American briefings and media framed the story as primarily US led.

In business & innovation today, global teams build breakthroughs  yet the press release, the TED Talk and the valuation narrative often position the American leader or company as the singular genius.

That’s the lesson for every leader, every organisation, every nation.

It was pointed out that the Americans had arrived after the battle had finished & that the fighting had been done by the New Zealanders. As the Italian campaign was being run by US Army command and the parade went ahead as planned.

In front came the US Marines, with a large banner bearing their emblem and the words ‘US Marines. Second to None’.

Behind them marched the New Zealanders carrying a large sheet with the word ‘None’.

It reveals deeper abt a certain American thought process that has repeated itself for decades.

“If we’re involved, we must have been decisive. If we show up, it’s our win.”

It’s not malice but a cultural operating system built on concept of exceptionalism.
We see this pattern everywhere . In WW II, US role often overshadows the fact that Soviet Union suffered 27 million dead, Britain stood alone for years & smaller Allies like New Zealand, Australia and Canada bled for every inch of ground from North Africa to Italy to the Pacific.

In coalition ops since, Korea, Gulf or Afghanistan where multinational forces did the heavy lifting in the shadows while American briefings and media framed the story as primarily US led.

In business & innovation today, global teams build breakthroughs yet the press release, the TED Talk and the valuation narrative often position the American leader or company as the singular genius.

That’s the lesson for every leader, every organisation, every nation.

Elite Predators (@elitepredatorss)

Met someone last evening. The guy is into the waste processing business and was earlier in the scrap collection business. I am generally very curious about how different businesses work and what kind of potential they have in the future. Heard some shocking facts:

- A seasoned mid-level scrap collector can earn up to ₹10K–₹12K per day. The game is to build a network of other waste collectors who can supply good-quality scrap.

- The industry is booming, and businesses in it are extremely cash-rich. You’re looking at millionaire-level annual numbers here.

I have always had a theory in my mind: are there groups across countries motivated to damage infrastructure and steal public assets meant for citizens’ safety and convenience?

Things like construction materials, especially iron, bridge railings, reflectors, metal signboards, benches at bus stops.

He didn’t confirm it outright, but he seemed uncomfortable. After gently pushing for a couple of minutes, he partially acknowledged it and said such activities can be politically motivated, and that if one digs deeper, a religious angle might also emerge.

After 1971, that was the fastest surrender of Pakistan. A new record for India. First fastest was 13 days in 1971, this one came in at around 88 hours.

The next surrender will come within the first couple of hours. The most logical thing would be to keep that hotline ringing till we sort every major military establishment inside Pakistan.

We can't kill the snake, but we can defang it to get 10-15 years of uninterrupted focus on national growth.
